dublinCore.dc:title: 119 HR 8357 IH: 6PPD Task Force Act
dublinCore.dc:rights: Pursuant to Title 17 Section 105 of the United States Code, this file is not subject to copyright protection and is in the public domain.

action.action-desc: Ms. Strickland (for herself, Ms. Randall, and Ms. Salinas) introduced the following bill; which was referred to the Committee on Science, Space, and Technology, and in addition to the Committees on Transportation and Infrastructure, Energy and Commerce, and Natural Resources, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned

form.official-title: To establish a task force to address the impacts of the chemical 6PPD and its by-product 6PPD-Q on coho salmon and the environment and to promote research and funding opportunities for an alternative to the chemical 6PPD, and for other purposes.

section.text: This Act may be cited as the 6PPD Task Force Act.

paragraph.text: 6PPD is defined as the chemical compound N-(1,3-dimethylbutyl)-N'- 11 phenyl-p-phenylenediamine.

6PPD is a chemical commonly used in all motor vehicle tires, military vehicle tires, and aircraft tires, contributing to passenger safety by preventing tires from degrading quickly and ensuring their durability.

As 6PPD in tires reacts with oxygen or ozone in the environment, it breaks down and transforms into compounds, including 6PPD- Stormwater runoff may cause tire and road wear particles containing 6PPD-Q to wash into streams, rivers, and other bodies of water that are habitats for aquatic species, such as Pacific salmon.
6PPD-from stormwater runoff is acutely toxic to adult and juvenile coho salmon (Oncorhynchus kisutch).

Private-sector efforts in the United States exist to develop potential alternatives to 6PPD that will not transform into 6PPD-and enter into waterways, leading to coho salmon mortality, and will not harm other wildlife species or people, while also prioritizing motor vehicle passenger safety and environmental impacts.
Finding a 6PPD alternative has national security, domestic supply chain, and environmental and human health implications and must be addressed in a timely manner.
Efforts to replace 6PPD must ensure continued compliance with vehicle safety and performance standards critical to passenger and public safety.

subsection.text: Not later than 60 days after the date of the enactment of this Act, the Secretary of Transportation, in consultation with the Administrator of the Environmental Protection Agency, shall, to the extent practicable, enter into an agreement with the National Academy of Sciences through which the Academy shall establish a task force to be known as the 6PPD Task Force (in this Act referred to as the Task Force).

paragraph.text: improve data and information sharing for research into an alternative to 6PPD among Federal agencies, research institutions, and State, Tribal, and local governments for the purpose of—

subparagraph.text: identifying the potential impacts of 6PPD on coho salmon, other aquatic species, human health, and the environment; and
mitigating the adverse effects of 6PPD on water quality, human health, and the environment;

paragraph.text: identify and recommend Federal funding opportunities to researchers, institutions of higher education, State governments, Tribal governments, local governments, tire manufacturers, chemical suppliers, tire additive manufacturers, and other entities to—

subparagraph.text: study the potential impacts of 6PPD, related compounds, and their degradation products, including 6PPD-, on Pacific salmon, other economically or ecologically valuable aquatic species, and human health; and
develop an alternative chemical to 6PPD, including scientific validation and scale-up activities to commercialize 6PPD alternatives;

paragraph.text: identify and provide—

subparagraph.text: recommendations for expediting the identification, toxicity, and performance testing of safer alternatives to 6PPD; and
opportunities to encourage the adoption of safer alternatives to 6PPD in tires available on the market;

paragraph.text: share with Federal agencies, research institutions, and State, Tribal, and local governments best practices, techniques, methodologies, and cost estimates for 6PPD mitigation efforts, including street sweeping and stormwater runoff treatment;

identify and provide research to Federal agencies, research institutions, and State, Tribal, and local governments regarding pathways not related to motor vehicle tires through which 6PPD exposure occurs, including exposure to 6PPD through—

subparagraph.text: reuse of tires, including artificial turf;

paragraph.text: share funding information, technical assistance, yearly reports, and resources in an online repository accessible to—

subsection.text: The Task Force shall be composed of the following members:

paragraph.text: A designee of the Secretary of Transportation.
A designee of the Administrator of the Environmental Protection Agency.
A designee of the Secretary of Agriculture.
A designee of the Director of the United States Geological Survey.
A designee of the Director of the United States Fish and Wildlife Service.
A designee of the Administrator of the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration.
A designee of the Director of the National Institutes of Health.
A designee of the Director of the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention.

Not less than four representatives from Tribal governments located on the western coast of the United States with current knowledge of 6PPD impacts on coho salmon appointed by the National Academy Sciences with consideration given to differences in regional perspectives and experience.
Not less than four representatives from the private sector appointed by the National Academy of Sciences, including—

subparagraph.text: two representatives from the tire manufacturing industry; and
two representatives from the tire chemical additive manufacturing industry.

paragraph.text: Not less than one representative from the environmental community appointed by the National Academy of Sciences.

Not less than two representatives, at least two of whom should be from different States, with expertise in environmental and public health from State government agencies with active 6PPD mitigation programs or that have documented 6PPD runoff impacts on aquatic species appointed by the National Academy of Sciences.
Not less than one representative from a university research institution with experience researching 6PPD and aquatic toxicology appointed by the National Academy of Sciences.

subsection.text: The members described in paragraphs (1) and (2) of subsection (c) shall serve as the Co-Chairs of the Task Force.
The Task Force shall meet not less than three times annually.

subsection.header: Inapplicability of the Federal Advisory Committee Act
subsection.text: Chapter 10 of title 5, United States Code (commonly known as the Federal Advisory Committee Act), shall not apply to the Task Force.

Not later than 180 days after the date of the enactment of this Act, and annually thereafter, the Task Force shall submit to the Committees on Transportation and Infrastructure and Energy and Commerce of the House of Representatives and the Committee on Environment and Public Works of the Senate and make publicly available a report that includes—

paragraph.text: findings and recommendations of the Task Force; and
a list of stakeholders and industry experts consulted by the Task Force.
The term 6PPD means the chemical compound N-(1,3-dimethylbutyl)-N’- 11 phenyl-p-phenylenediamine.
The term 6PPD-Q means 6PPD-quinone.
